text.heading: NECO and standards
text.p: SIR: The discrimination by Cross River State University against NECO SSCE Certificates should not be condemned wholesale without thorough investigation. Anyone who has been following the activities of NECO and its staff would confirm that the examination board has sacrificed academic standards on the altar of lucrative business. Remember, the Federal Government used its might to create NECO by fiat and so, became an extension of the Federal Civil Service.

Both NECO and WAEC operate the same syllabus but there is a wide gulf between them. While WAEC looks beyond Nigeria by setting standards comparable to the London, Cambridge and other international examination bodies, NECO is not in competition because it is in a world of her own. Most NECO state offices are fiefdoms meant to collect tributes from conquered schools, particularly the hapless private schools. The staff know full well whether they discharge their duties or not the Federal Government crude oil account is wide open to cater for their salaries.

Take the distribution of practical instructions to schools for instance. These are vital materials meant for the subject teachers only, that ought to be distributed by hand in sealed envelopes, emphasizing the importance attached. But NECO distributes these instructions in sheets picked by any representatives of schools from the floor of their offices or at times from C.I.Es’ offices.

Their exam supervisors are selected not on the basis of qualifications but to the highest bidder. Most seasoned examiners avoid their marking exercise as they (NECO) prefer cheap and inexperienced ones in order to maximise profit at the detriment of standards. These and many other myriad of problems of NECO which are selfinflicted cannot make the body efficient and serve the purpose for which it was established.

My candid suggestion is to encourage the University of Ibadan, Ahmadu Bello University and University of Nigeria, Nsukka to set up examination boards similar to WAEC. NECO should be scrapped completely because it lacks credibility, good legacies and should not be allowed to influence such new boards. As long as NECO exists unsupervised and unaccountable, so shall it contribute partly to the deteriorating educational standards in the country.
